item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, I've been working my way through Roman scips today and this one is my favourite. The idea of a lion being a general and becoming Emperor is just… I just love that. The article is a perfect length, and that 'extract' from a historical account adds so much to it by framing it in the language of the day. I can only imagine what it would be like living under him, what he would be like in person (in lion?), and what else weird stuff happened while he was around. Him worshipping the cat god is perfect. What if he fell in love with Egypt like Marc Antony did?

Kinda disappointed that so many people disliked it. I get that the Romans having a lion as Emperor seems unbelievable, because we would never accept it nowadays, but my headcanon is that there was a lot more openness about the supernatural in that time period. Like, we would never accept a talking animal as our leader today because we don't believe in talking animals and the supernatural. But I can imagine that in Roman times there were no organisations like the Foundation that suppressed the anomalous, and so the Romans saw a lot of supernatural stuff that meant that a talking lion wasn't seen as very strange. "Wow, a talking lion, another of the wonders of the gods." Having him be a high-standing figure might be exotic to some people, even if resented by others. It was only afterwards that information got suppressed, and the supernatural disappeared from the public eye. I know none of this is stated, but it helps it make sense, to me at least.

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
The first image could not be source reliably and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

The second image is property of P. Chapuis / MAFB, Copyright Hypogees. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First I learn Catherine the Great didn't die when her custom made "belly saddle" broke, now this.
All the best parts of equine history are false. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
and others still claimed that a spurned lover had sought his revenge against the emperor…

Ouch, poor guy… XD

Overall, I like the fact the Foundation mostly needs to… keep thing as they are, since most redaction and hiding was already done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think it being a lion is such a big problem. As an anomalous entity, the beast could have any other undocumented property that explains its accomplishments. Actually, it does have at least an astonishing learning speed if it managed to seize the throne at just five years. This isn't much to my taste, but I can accept this.

The thousands of years of suppression on this info also makes some sense. Though I do agree with others that it is far harder than it seems to completely hide history, to a degree I can accept this level of success for the sake of a good article, if I need to. But in this case, I don't.

My problem here is with the entire classification of this as a skip. There is nothing to be contained. The lion died 18 centuries ago. Even if there is evidence of it being a lion, it would pose no threat to normalcy. People would either dismiss it and interpret another way, or would be seen as wacky. Belief in the supernatural is already a common feature of normalcy, there is more people with some belief of this kind than the other way around.

Unless something anomalous remained until recently, the Foundation have no need or reason to classify this even as neutralized. It would be far too costly for no benefit whatsoever, even more considering that this lion is hardly the only anomaly that happened in the past.

item.title: I like it
item.content:encoded:
I don't care it's kinda implausible. I like it.

I came here from http://scp-wiki.wikidot.com/wrong-proposal

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Shiiiit, I was willing to bet money that this was a Dmatix article, on account of the Roman history focus. But talking animals should have been a dead giveaway about the author.

As for the article itself, I actually like it quite a bit. The goofy stuff about single combat made me chuckle, and I'm always one for historical conspiracies. The fact that it is so bonkers and implausible actually made it work quite well for me! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just want to say this is my second favorite skip after 140.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, he didn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The main image here is uploaded as huge and then just shrunk down, which makes it load slowly. Would anyone mind if I shrunk it down and reuploaded it? Smapti?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neutral. The premise is just too wacky for me to take seriously, but it's a well written article that approaches SCPs from a fresh angle.

Upvoted after reconsideration.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: =oD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
His is probably better than Mitt Romney's, anyway.

(ba-dum-TISH)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd vote for him if he had a good grasp of foreign policy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
poor Iggy, no one believes in him.

How about this? You agree to vote the Rept-publican ticket and I'll promise to start a debate on your next article too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edit: *tail*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fuck. I actually kind of like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, HAHAHAHAH….that's hilarious. They probably nicknamed Rome as Narnia…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When his legionaries run into battle, do they shout "FOR ASLAN!"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have a suggestion. Bearing in mind that the Romans would not have let a talking lion become emperor, if the lion was as cunning as you claim, could he not have used a sort of human "puppet"? A "face" for the throne? To me, the suggestion that the Romans would knowingly allow a lion to become emperor is ludricous. It would be much more believable to me at least if it was somehow hinted that he was emperor in secret. In other words, a human was on the throne, but the real emperor was the lion. It makes more sense if the public had never known he was a lion. However, this suggestion in no way implies that I would like this SCP any better if it were altered. It's just plain kooky.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough. Everyone have their own pet peeve when it comes to SCPs, and this one just managed to hit nearly every single one of mine. Can't be helped.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As noted, the Foundation aren't the ones who started covering it up. It's been being suppressed since shortly after the lion died 1800 years ago. Caesaras, the history that I "quoted" in the addendum, is an actual text that was lost in its entirety by 400 or so. All the Foundation is doing is making sure that any new historical evidence that comes to light, and the inherent "ludicrousness" of the concept makes that fairly easy for them to do - there are actual Roman coins with lions on them, for instance, so the coins bearing Septimius Leo's profile can be written off, and presumably there were also coins portraying him with a human face as well (I can imagine Leo wanting himself to be portrayed as Roman-born in order to give his reign more legitimacy).

I'm obviously not going to change your mind or out-argue you on historical grounds, but that's just the logic I was using when I wrote this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Most of those points were mentioned (and dissected) during our discussion, so there isn't much point of repeating them. My two biggest problems with this article are as follows:

1. The idea that people of any background or time period after the agricultural revolution would accept an animal of any kind to be their ruler (a political ruler, not an idol or god, which is different) , not to mention an advance culture like Principate period Rome, breaks my suspension of disbelief. Troubled times in Rome or no, this would simply not happen. True, Rome had many non-Italian emperors, such as Philip the Arab or Marcus Aurelius, but the differences between them and a big talking cat are incalculably big. One of the biggest reasons humanity moved from a predominantly animistic religious order to a polytheistic one is because they could no longer accept animals as equals. Romans might have been fairly religious, but not nearly to such a degree.

2. The Foundation being at all capable of covering this up. The emperor was a enormously well known figure even in places such as India, which never had a legion anywhere near them (Roman coins were used even there as acceptable currency), and Principate period Rome is one of the best documented periods in history in general. To keep something like this a secret, considering the amount of people the lion interacted with, is impossible, especially when you are doing this more than a thousand years in the future.

I can accept a lot of things from an SCP article, but this is way, way too ludicrous in so many different ways.

(note, this is coming from someone who is in the field, so take that as you will. Suspension of disbelief might be easier here for people not as familiar with the period)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why do all the best arguments about my SCPs happen while i'm asleep?

I'm surprised this one has been so divisive - it's my lowest-rated SCP to date, but I'm kind of fond of it, and clearly enough people like it that its net rating is still positive. Just to comment on a few things;

-The Romans didn't just "accept" a talking lion as emperor, he seized power in a civil war. Wikipedia will tell you the real Septimius Severus became emperor after what was known as "the Year of Five Emperors", where several rulers were killed in rapid succession. His immediate predecessor, as noted in the article, was assassinated by the Praetorian Guard itself, who then auctioned the throne off to the man he defeated in the subsequent war.

-It's not as though the Roman Empire hadn't had foreign or non-aristocratic rulers by this point. The real Septimius was African. Pertinax (the one killed by the Praetorian Guard) was the son of a freed slave. Marcus Aurelius was a Spaniard. In later centuries, they had Arabs and Syrians as emperors. Granted, none of those people were talking lions, but I was thinking along the lines of the poster above who suggested that a talking lion in the company of an emperor would be seen as an omen of the gods, or that it might be considered to be a god itself - and throwing a god into the arena probably isn't a good idea.

- Could a talking iguana get elected president? Probably not, but then the number of people who got to "vote" for emperor was pretty small, and in our country's religious tradition talking animals are more closely associated with Satan than anything else. :)

- Gladiators did sometimes achieve pretty high social status. IIRC, Commodus' lover (who eventually assassinated him) was a gladiator.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is well-written (as usual, for you) and the bits of actual Roman history are cool, but I just can't buy the main premise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It seems this is an unreconcilable difference of opinion.

All I can say for certain is that I personally would vote for a lion that was intelligent, erudite, and presented himself in the context of a stirring narrative. The inherent fact that he is non-human would make me more likely to support him as a leader, not less. I have learned that I am not educated enough to speak for the Romans, but I think that some portion of the american voting public would agree with me, for the novelty factor if nothing else.

But if you would not, then that is why we have a voting system in the first place. Both in politics and on the wiki.

Edit: I wonder what his platform would be? "An antelope in every pot", maybe? Would he instinctively try to kill the children of the incumbent, like the offspring of a displaced pride leader?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
if one is prepared to accept that a lion can talk, I do not find it an unreasonable step too far to also accept that it has the skills and temprament to rise to political power.

If one is prepared to believe that a lion can talk, one should also be prepared to believe that it might have nothing interesting or intelligent to say. ("How much longer 'til Snooki is on TV?")

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rats and mice do have manipulative skill. They can push buttons and levers, pile up available objects to form steps and platforms, and possibly use sticks to trigger traps safely (that last one is a controversial study that hasn't been repeatable). Your point holds for lions though, no useful hands at all.

I still say that a lion who can strategize will always beat either a lion who cannot or the much slower, weaker human. But, at it's root, where in a "Muhamed Ali VS Mike Tyson" debate. You can't logic out a winner, only a real fight would settle it.

And wouldn't I pay to see that fight!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See, that's not what the article is arguing. The article says a talking lion can become emperor, the single highest position in Rome, the single most powerful political force in the world. To be honest, I wouldn't even buy the lion as a minor military commander (like a Tribunus Militum), or anything above a curiosity to the ancient Romans, but to be asked to believe a talking lion could become Principate, and that the Foundation would be able to hide it no less, is taking my suspension of disbelief behind the shed and shooting it in the head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are worse retirements, i suppose.

I think you give Big Science too much credit. As a citizen I am ashamed to admit it, but science and scientists are more of a liability in politics than a power. Something about that whole "insisting on proof" thing just doesn't seem to work well with modern speech-ifying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Me too, especially if his horse was Mr. Ed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bite your tongue! Of course it was a true story. How could the uplifting tail of Mrs. Brisby be anything less than gospel! :P

But I take the point, I'm drifting off topic because I'm having fun, I do that a lot.

All I started out to say is that; if one is prepared to accept that a lion can talk, I do not find it an unreasonable step too far to also accept that it has the skills and temprament to rise to political power.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed. Truly interesting, discussing slavery, gladiators, and money in Ancient Rome.
And You're right. I myself had neglected freedmen. What people don't seem to realize, is, aside from the blood and guts, being a gladiator, for some people, was an extremely lucrative profession.

E: Not yet, but when I do start college, History will indeed be my major.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On the contrary, every nut job PETA member in America would vote for Iggy, just saying, lol.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …crap. I already placed an order for the custom suit with the little power tie and the hole for his tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: NIMH was not a documentary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1- simply not true. Humanity evolved to use its mental capacity to it best- a lion has no hands to use tools, for example and neither would the NIMH mice. A lion, even one with human intelligence, can't use a weapon, write, ride a chariot or a horse without specially made instruments, which, as previously stated, the Romans would have no real reason to give it.

2. Saying that a country would accept an iguana president that was just discoverd a few years ago is ridiculous. Going back to historical examples, black slaves, who were just as human as the white population, took centuries to get free and even more to get equal rights, and even today with a black presidents there is still considerable discrimination- and they are human beings equal in every respect, not a talking lizard.

(this is to wishun)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Your forgetting some gladiators did in fact buy their own freedom. These days, when people think of slavery, they think of slavery as it was in the Southern US; cruel, hard, inescapeable. Not so with the Romans. Slavery for the Romans was a bit more complex. Roman slaves could and did earn their own money. In fact, some masters actually paid their slaves.Slaves could hold a separate job. Most Roman slaves, unless they were owned by a particularly harsh master, were treated like members of the family. They were expected to work, yes, but not quite in the way we imagine slavery today. Most Romans treated their slaves well.
Now, I won't pretend to assume that slaves who became gladiators were treated well, and I'm certainly sure they weren't treated like the members of anyone's family, however, there still remains the fact that most slaves were in fact paid some form of salary. I won't say that all gladiators were paid salaries, however, I'm certain that all the really famous gladiators, the ones who became ancient megastars, were paid salaries. I'm sure Spartacus was paid a salary also. This brings me to the last unique point of Roman slavery; all slaves had the right under Roman law to purchase their freedom if they wanted to and had earned enough money to pay their master. As popular as some gladiators became, and as much money I'm sure those popular gladiators earned, I'm quite certain that more than a few did in fact succeed in buying their freedom, and retired richly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. A political career is inherently not a position of seclusion, they only succeeded because they could hide and stay off the radar. Imagine NIMH if one of the rats went to a news reporter and was like "Hi, I'm a rat from a small mouse village behind that specific bush, I want to be the president", either that little village would be wiped out of the rat be crushed under the reporters foot (because they freaked out.)

2. Political skill isn't all that big a decider these days, especially not if the candidate is some freak that escaped from a top-secret lab.

Sure, the PETA people could make cases and debates to keep Iggy free or at least to keep him alive, but the scientific community in general would be too powerful a foe for any hippy to overcome. Iggy would spend his days in a lab/zoo, pining for the oval officed while he eats mangoes and discusses politics with his care taker.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You have to be 35 years of age to be POTUS (in addition to the othe requirements). Panthera leo doesn't get that old (nor, I suspect, do iguanas).

(Edit: I checked. The oldest known iguana in captivity died at 32. Coincidentally, iguanas and lions in captivity have similar lifespans. You want a long-lived animal, you want a coral, a lobster, a koi fish, a sponge, a bowhead whale or a tortoise.)

Pity for Aslan. I'm sure there's a Christ-lion for President natural constituency.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad you're having a good time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's also easy to forget some gladiators were freemen, not slaves. True, they were a minority, but they existed. Naturally, they fought for the money and fame, rather than for freedom.

As for slaves, indeed, no former slave could become emperor. Former slaves were called "Libertus", or freedmen, and were somewhere between a slave and a freeman (or even a citizen). Their sons, however, were fully free, and were often made citizens.

It's nice we can have an interesting discussion based on a stupid article, eh?

E: Are you a history major, by the way? Always nice to meet a fellow historian.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are two separate points I'm thinking on, and reading back I've muddled them together.

1) intelligent animals would, by definition, be more capable than humans. The only advantage humans have over any animal is our intelligence, and that has been neutralized. NIMH rats succeed despite the best human efforts. They were isolationists, just wanting to be left alone. But there is no reason to think they would have been less succesful when pursuing some other philosophy.

2) An intelligent animal may also be a savvy and politically adroit animal. They wouldn't necessarily have to be, but the possibility for the talent exists. Enough political skill can overcome any initially poor reception. Not that long ago, people who were not racist themselves said the country wouldn't tolerate a black president. In a few more elections, it will be a female president. A few after that and it will be a gay president. A few more after that, an uplifted lizard. Sufficient skill will cut down the number of necessary cycles for any given step.

My Iggy starts in a lab. But then he escapes and/or recruits a sympathetic doctor. Once he becomes a public figure, there is no getting him back in that cage. The sheer number of PETA-esque activists would make it physically impossible, not to mention socio-political suicide.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Indeed he could not. However, the above people seem to forget that a small number of gladiators did in fact retire. They way did this was quite simple; they bought their own freedom from slavery. Bear in mind, any gladiator that did so had to have been incredibly popular, so much so that he was able to earn enough money to buy freedom.
Another note, not all gladiators were viewed the same way we view porn stars today. Some gladiators were extremely famous, to the point that at least one well known gladiator actually received five marriage proposals…while he was still a gladiator. And yes, that does mean that at least five women wanted to marry a celebrity who was technically a slave. According to records, he was considered the most handsome gladiator there was at that time. So, could a gladiator retire? Yes. Did many of them retire? No. And there is certainly no way a former slave could become emperor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I could see that if the humans in NIMH were just fine with the intelligent rat/mouse civilization, but in the film they actively try to capture them and for the most part care even less about the ramifications of intelligent animal abuse than we actually would.

That's the thing here, I can fully see a talking animal being taken seriously, but I can't see them being treated as equal humans. We're just not that united as a species, we'd see a talking turtle and have that sucker in a closely controlled and monitored cage until we understand it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Iggy was born in Arizona, as part of a zoo breeding program. His birth is at least as well documented as Obama's. Show me where in the constitution it says the president has to be a harless ape. :D Keep in mind that Michael Moore once ran a ficus for senate, and it won the popular vote.

Would his "heritage" be an issue" I assumed so! But a good politician thrives on controversy. With a very little bit of spin, Iggy could be portrayed as the underdog, fighting against bigotry and prejudice, overcomign great hardship and personal adversity so that he can humbly serve the country he loves.

I like this character I've built. I think I'll write a children's book about him. Showing kids the election process via a boggle-eyed lizard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The NIMH mice/rats were lab-enhanced and not naturally occuring, yes. But the parallel I was going for is that these Algernons escape en masse and maintain a free society, despite the best efforts of their human handlers. Even though the people watching them knew for a fact that they were intelligent.

I find it a much more realistic vision of what human-intelligence animals would be capable of than the hijinks of most talking animals.

I think Disney, Bugs Bunny, etc. have cemented in our collective unconcious that talking animals are goofy things, pale and laughable imitations of humans. And it is this entrenched preconception that, in my own uninformed opinion, is keeping some from enjoying this piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, well that's cool, plus I learned something and knowledge is power.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hmmm…I seem to be out of my depth.
Being no true historian, I took at face value the anecdotal testimony I've gotten from history majors, to the effect that most gladiators retired to lives of at least minor celebrity, the justification being that training gladiators is expensive. Too expensive to be killing them all the time. I bow to your obviously more thorough (in that you have done any) research.

Leo could not have risen to power via the arena.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Did gladiators get to retire? I was under the impression that they just got dog-piled in tougher and tougher opponents until they got executed.

Depended on the gladiator, as well on when in Roman history you're talking about. They were slaves, so (assuming they were good enough to survive and weren't criminals where being sentenced to the arena was a creative form of the death penalty) it depended on the whims of their masters. Spartacus is probably the most famous example of someone who was once a gladiator, then was retired. He didn't stop being a slave, the rebellion he led notwithstanding.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But, at worst, Leo would be the best fighter to ever enter the Arena. He's smarter than any other animal they match him up with, and vastly stronger/faster than any human opponent. He's untouchable, and he's smart enough to make a show of it, quickly becoming a crowd favorite.

Not necessarily. Leo vs. other animals? Maybe (but intellect does not guarantee survival in the gladiatorial games). Leo vs. human opponents? Some would definitely fall (normal lions did kill gladiators), but remember, human opponents tended to be armed and armored, and the lions that historically lived in southern Europe were hunted to extinction during this time period. He'd not be untouchable. Crowd favorite? I'd buy that, but the gladiatorial games were ruthless, bloody, and even the champions didn't have a very long life expectancy.

Leo, at minimum retires as a respected celebrity, and if he's as savvy as this article implies, parlays his celebrity into political power.

Gladiators rarely lived to retire. Most (though not all) were slaves or criminals, and those who weren't were generally marginalized in society. They tended to be regarded with the same double-edged fame/infamy as modern-day porn stars. (The successful ones also tended to be sex symbols, resulting in deaths from venereal diseases - whether this would apply in Leo's case or not could be debated, so that may be beside the point). To my knowledge of Roman political history, gladiators were NEVER able to leverage their position for political power (Spartacus was a former gladiator but still a slave when he led his uprising). Sure, some of the emperors occasionally dabbled in the arena, but they generally rigged the process so they were in little danger - and this practice was generally disapproved by both contemporary political elites and contemporary commentators, no matter how much the masses enjoyed it. If Leo had been a gladiator, and had tried to leverage political power, the response of the emperor would probably have been essentially "hahahahahahaNO." Consider the following passage from Wikipedia:

The phenomenon of the "volunteer" gladiator is more problematic. All contracted volunteers, including those of equestrian and senatorial class, were legally enslaved by their auctoratio because it involved their potentially lethal submission to a master. Nor does the citizen or free volunteer's "professional" status translate into modern terms. All arenarii (those who appeared in the arena) were "infames by reputation", a form of social dishonour which excluded them from most of the advantages and rights of citizenship. Payment for such appearances compounded their infamia. The legal and social status of even the most popular and wealthy auctorati was thus marginal at best. They could not vote, plead in court nor leave a will; unless they were manumitted, their lives and property belonged to their masters. Nevertheless, there is evidence of informal if not entirely lawful practices to the contrary. Some "unfree" gladiators bequeathed money and personal property to wives and children, possibly via a sympathetic owner or familia; some had their own slaves and gave them their freedom. One gladiator was even granted "citizenship" to several Greek cities of the Eastern Roman world. (Emphasis added)

Seriously, this idea just doesn't work. When Caligula appointed his horse consul, nobody took Incitatus seriously as a political force. It was seen as either a sign of the emperor's madness, or as a prank and mockery (implying both that a Senator's duties could be done by a mere horse, and/or that the Senators were all of lower status than a mere horse), or some combination of the two. Nobody actually took the idea seriously; they just couldn't really do a whole lot about it since Caligula was the tyrant in charge. (That is, until the Praetorian Guard was paid off into assassinating the emperor.)

Long story short: downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Think more "Secret of NIMH" than "Ratatouille".

It's been a while since I've seen NIMH, but wasn't that whole event because we were fucking around with the mice? I might be mistaken but I remember the lady mouse getting stuck in a cage in a lab.

Leo, at minimum retires as a respected celebrity, and if he's as savvy as this article implies, parlays his celebrity into political power.

Did gladiators get to retire? I was under the impression that they just got dog-piled in tougher and tougher opponents until they got executed. I imaging that a talking lion would just be up against bigger and tougher challenges as the days went by until he was screaming for mercy (wouldn't that just be the perfect power trip for a Roman Emperor, making his most fearsome opponent scream for mercy from the blood mud floor of the Colosseum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think you are vastly underestimating human xenophobia

"Vastly" doesn't even cover it. 18% of U.S. respondents in a Gallup poll said they would be unwilling to vote for their own party's candidate if the party nominated someone who was Mormon. 30% if gay or lesbian. 40% if Muslim. 43% if atheist. (Ref.) The poll didn't ask, but I'm guessing a non-primate would have even less support still.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A non American can't even become president, not to mention a non-human. I think you are vastly underestimating human xenophobia- people are exclusive and suspicious even of people similar to them in every respect save minor things like the melanin count in their skins-how would they feel about an animal- a being they are used to eat or hunt, that they consider inferior to them by its very nature, giving them orders? They would never accept it.

For example, if that lion was put in an arena, do you think they'll let him fight fair battles? They'd simply give him more outrageous odds every time- even a talking lion wouldn't stand much of a chance against twenty armed opponents.

And in addition, like MrCobalt said, something like this would be utterly impossible to hide- which is what this article stands on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're not giving Iggy, and by extension Leo, enough credit.

A talking animal is inherently superior to humans. Because it has human level inteligence and the superior natural abilities of an animal. Think more "Secret of NIMH" than "Ratatouille".

Let's refocus on Leo. Let's say the emperor decided he would be more entertaining in the arena than as a living mockery of a pompous co-worker. This is perfectly plausible. But, at worst, Leo would be the best fighter to ever enter the Arena. He's smarter than any other animal they match him up with, and vastly stronger/faster than any human opponent. He's untouchable, and he's smart enough to make a show of it, quickly becoming a crowd favorite. Leo, at minimum retires as a respected celebrity, and if he's as savvy as this article implies, parlays his celebrity into political power.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dunno about you, but something like this seems like it would be out of reach for the Foundation. I mean, their job is to contain anomalous entities and hide them from public knowledge, and a historical fact like "Septimus Severus was a sapient talking lion" would probably be beyond the Foundation's reach.

Also, for some reason this made me think of when Caligula elected his favorite horse to the Senate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love history, and especially the Romans. Severus was one of the best emperors they ever had. Why did you turn him into a damn talking lion? You totally got this idea from Nero's horse, right?

EDIT I meant Caligula's horse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
He'd win in a land-slide.

If by "win in a land-slide" you mean "be studied and physically/mentally picked apart for clues to his origins" you might be right, however he would never win presidency and he would never get close to such a position. The most likely fate of Iggy would be one of torment, legal/moral debates, and in the end his corpse would be pulled apart for anything that might make him unique.

The lion on the other hand would probably be put into Gladiator matches until he died, probably roaring and screaming at the applauding crowd while a human emperor laughed at him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Disney has ruined your imagination. A talking animal is not a silly, goofy, or whimsical creature per se.

A talking iguana calls a press conference, delivers an impassioned and eloquent speech about it's life of humiliation and exploitation at the hands of it's circus ringleader and/or scientists. Iggy appeals to our sense of justice, all he wants is the respect and dignity that is every thinking beings inalienable right! Please, won't you let him join this greatest of all nations, this grand historical experiment, AMERICA!
He'd win in a land-slide.

And I wasn't saying the Romans were stupid, quite the opposite. I believe that the Romans would look at the evidence before them (a talking lion with political ambitions and the drive, skill, and connections to make it happen) and react appropriately (be ersepctful to the thing what could kill you and may very well end up being your boss).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The chance of the Romans accepting an animal as Emperor is about the same as of a modern country accepting one- zero. The Romans weren't some barely educated bunch of farmers and hunters- they created a massive Empire with a complex civil and military structure and quite an advance culture- some emperors were mad, sure, but they rose to power because of the result of politics, familial power and other similar reasons. None of them was a bloody talking animal. The Romans (especially those of the late Principate) would most likely treat a talking animal like a modern society would- as a curiosity to be kept for amusement or to be studied, and certainly not as a valid political figure.

The very notion is beyond absurd to me. Imagine the president of the United States being a talking iguana, because it makes as much sense as this.
